stories.title: Implement TP2 Form — Employee Irrevocable BIK/VOLA Election for PCB Inclusion
stories.description: LHDN allows employees to make an irrevocable election within a tax year to include Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) and the Value of Living Accommodation (VOLA) as part of their monthly remuneration for MTD/PCB purposes by submitting Form TP2 to the employer. Without TP2 handling, BIK and VOLA are excluded from PCB by default, meaning employees who prefer MTD spreading over lump-sum year-end payment have no mechanism to elect this. The employer must honour the election, adjust PCB computation for the remainder of the year, and retain the TP2 for 7 years.
acceptanceCriteria: A TP2 Election child table or linked DocType on the Employee record captures: election date, BIK monthly value, VOLA monthly value, employer approval status, and effective month
Once approved by employer, the BIK + VOLA amounts are added to the employee's gross remuneration base used in PCB calculation for all subsequent payroll runs within the same assessment year
The election is flagged irrevocable — system prevents reversal within the same assessment year under the same employer
BIK/VOLA amounts added via TP2 do NOT appear in gross salary on the payslip or EA Form gross salary field; they only affect the PCB engine input
System generates a TP2 record PDF with employee name, IC number, employer name, election amounts, and approval signature block
TP2 records are flagged with a 7-year retention requirement in the payroll audit log
technicalNotes: LHDN PCB Specification 2025: BIK/VOLA elected via TP2 is added to gross remuneration for MTD calculation only (not for payslip/EA gross salary)
TP2 is not submitted to LHDN — it is an internal employer record required to be retained 7 years under Section 82 ITA
Reference: LHDN Specification for Computerised PCB Calculation 2025 (https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf)
The irrevocability clause means the PCB engine must lock the BIK/VOLA addend for assessment year once set
dependencies: Implement Benefits-in-Kind (BIK) Prescribed Value Calculation Module

stories.title: Store Employee TIN on Employee DocType and Validate for e-PCB Plus Upload
stories.description: LHDN's e-PCB Plus system (mandatory from 2025) requires each employee's Tax Identification Number (TIN) to be included in the e-Data PCB upload file. LHDN explicitly instructs employers to update employee TIN details in their payroll system prior to submission — incomplete or missing TIN data causes the upload to be rejected. Currently the app stores the employee's NRIC/Passport but may not capture TIN as a separate mandatory field validated before CP39 export.
acceptanceCriteria: A `custom_employee_tin` field exists on the Employee DocType and is included in the custom_field.json fixture
The CP39 / e-Data PCB export includes the employee TIN column in the correct position per LHDN e-PCB Plus file specification
Payroll processing raises a validation warning (not hard block) if any employee in the pay period has a blank TIN
A pre-submission validation report lists all employees with missing TIN, so HR can bulk-update before CP39 export
TIN format is validated: Malaysian TIN is either IG/SG/OG/D/C prefix followed by 11 digits
technicalNotes: e-PCB Plus upload file (e-Data PCB .txt format) requires employee TIN in field position per LHDN e-PCB Plus specification
LHDN notice: 'Employers using e-Data PCB are requested to update the TIN details of their employees in their payroll system'
Reference: LHDN e-PCB Plus Transition (https://pandahrms.com/lhdns-early-announcement-transition-to-e-pcb-plus-system/)
Malaysian TIN formats: IG (individual), SG (salary earner), OG (business), D (company), C (company) — payroll employees will be IG or SG prefix
dependencies: Fix CP39 CSV Format — Add All Mandatory LHDN e-PCB Plus Columns

stories.title: EPF Three-Account (75/15/10) Contribution Split Display on Payslip and i-Akaun Export
stories.description: Since 11 May 2024, EPF restructured contributions into three accounts: Akaun Persaraan (75%), Akaun Sejahtera (15%), and Akaun Fleksibel (10%). Employer Borang A submissions and the i-Akaun employer portal must reflect the account split. Payslips should inform employees how their EPF deduction is allocated across the three accounts to support financial transparency and match what members see in i-Akaun (Member). This is distinct from the mandatory contribution rate story — it is the allocation presentation and export correctness.
acceptanceCriteria: Payslip shows EPF total deduction broken down into three sub-lines: Akaun Persaraan (75%), Akaun Sejahtera (15%), Akaun Fleksibel (10%) computed from the employee's EPF deduction
The i-Akaun employer CSV export file encodes the 75/15/10 allocation correctly per KWSP i-Akaun upload specification (where applicable)
The allocation applies only to members under age 55 (members at 55+ have different account rules and remain on the old two-account structure)
Rounding of the three sub-amounts sums exactly to the total EPF deduction — remainder ringgit allocated to Akaun Persaraan
EA Form EPF field continues to show the total employee EPF contribution (not split), as per CP8A specification
technicalNotes: EPF three-account restructuring effective 11 May 2024 per KWSP announcement
75/15/10 split applies to all new contributions post-May 2024 for members under 55
Members aged 55 and above remain on two-account structure (Account 1 / Account 2) under transitional provisions
Reference: KWSP Akaun Fleksibel Account Restructuring (https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/member/account-centre/account-restructuring)
dependencies: Generate EPF i-Akaun Electronic Upload File

stories.title: Enforce ITA Section 82 7-Year Payroll Tax Record Retention (Separate from MOHR 6-Year)
stories.description: Section 82 of the Income Tax Act 1967 requires employers to keep all payroll-related tax records (EA forms, CP39 files, Borang E acknowledgements, TP1/TP2/TP3 forms, MTD payment receipts) for 7 years from the end of the assessment year. This is a separate and longer obligation than the Employment Act's 6-year payroll register requirement (already covered). Non-compliance means LHDN can assess tax on a best-judgment basis and impose penalties. The system must enforce a 7-year retention lock on LHDN-related payroll records distinct from the MOHR 6-year lock.
acceptanceCriteria: A `lhdn_retention_expiry_date` field is set on LHDN-related records (EA Form, CP39 run, Borang E, TP1/TP2/TP3) as assessment_year + 7 years
Attempting to delete or archive LHDN records before their `lhdn_retention_expiry_date` raises a blocking validation error citing 'ITA Section 82 — 7-year retention'
A scheduled report runs annually listing LHDN records eligible for deletion (past their 7-year expiry) for HR Manager review
The retention lock applies independently of the Employment Act 6-year lock on payslips — an EA Form for YA2019 (issued Feb 2020) cannot be deleted before Feb 2027
System clearly labels the retention reason as 'ITA Section 82' vs 'Employment Act S.60A' so audit trails distinguish the two obligations
technicalNotes: ITA Section 82: records must be kept 7 years from the end of the year to which any income relates
LHDN Public Ruling No. 5/2000 (revised) defines what constitutes 'sufficient records' including payroll records, EA forms, and MTD receipts
Reference: ANC Group explanation of Section 82 (https://ancgroup.biz/keep-sufficient-records-for-7-years/)
Existing story 'Implement Payroll Wage Register with 6-Year Retention' covers MOHR Employment Act obligation — this story is additive for LHDN-specific records
dependencies: Implement Payroll Wage Register with 6-Year Retention and LHDN/MOHR Audit Export

stories.title: CP500 Bi-Monthly Advance Tax Installment Awareness Panel for Directors with Non-Salary Income
stories.description: Directors and sole proprietors who have non-employment income (dividends, rental, business profits) receive LHDN CP500 notices requiring 6 bi-monthly advance tax installments in March, May, July, September, November, and January. ERPNext payroll operates the PCB deduction for salary, but does not track whether the same director also has CP500 obligations outside payroll. HR/payroll admins need visibility into which directors are CP500 payers so they can advise against over-deducting PCB on salary and help directors avoid double-paying or underpaying. LHDN intensified CP500 issuance in 2025-2026.
acceptanceCriteria: A `cp500_payer` boolean flag and `cp500_annual_installment_amount` field can be set on the Director/Employee record
If `cp500_payer = True`, the payroll summary for that director includes an advisory note: 'Employee has CP500 obligations — confirm PCB deduction does not exceed actual tax liability'
A CP500 Installment Tracker child table on the Employee allows recording installment due dates (Mar/May/Jul/Sep/Nov/Jan) and payment reference numbers
Dashboard widget on LHDN Payroll Workspace shows directors with upcoming CP500 installment due dates within next 30 days
System does not auto-deduct CP500 (it is paid directly by the director to LHDN) — it is advisory and tracking only
technicalNotes: CP500 is issued by LHDN to individuals with non-salary income; 6 bi-monthly installments across the calendar year
The payment schedule: installment months are March, May, July, September, November, January
Revision of CP500 amount: first revision by 30 June, second by 31 October of the assessment year
Reference: LHDN CP500 guide (https://www.ajobthing.com/resources/blog/cp500-installment-pay-tax-malaysia)

stories.title: e-Lejar Employer Tax Account Ledger Reconciliation Link and PCB Payment Verification
stories.description: LHDN's e-Lejar application (accessible via MyTax portal) allows employers to check their employer tax account ledger transactions — including PCB remittances received, assessments raised, and refunds — at any time. Currently the lhdn_payroll_integration app tracks submission references but has no way to cross-check whether LHDN's ledger matches the amounts the employer remitted. Employers frequently discover discrepancies (e.g., payments credited to wrong month, rejected submissions not re-submitted) only during year-end Borang E reconciliation. A link to e-Lejar and a monthly reconciliation workflow would close this gap.
acceptanceCriteria: LHDN Developer Tools page includes a direct link to e-Lejar (https://mytax.hasil.gov.my) with instructions for employer ledger access
A 'PCB Ledger Reconciliation' panel allows HR to input LHDN's e-Lejar monthly credited amounts and compare against the sum of CP39 submissions for the same period
Discrepancies (difference > RM0) are flagged with a yellow warning showing the month, expected amount, and LHDN-credited amount
Reconciliation records are stored per assessment year and can be exported as a reconciliation report for audit purposes
System generates a monthly reminder alert on the 20th of each month prompting HR to verify e-Lejar balance against the previous month's CP39 payment
technicalNotes: e-Lejar is accessible at https://mytax.hasil.gov.my — employers log in with their employer TIN
e-Lejar shows up to 10 years of tax ledger history per LHDN information
No public API for e-Lejar programmatic access — integration is manual entry + comparison, not API pull
Reference: e-Lejar LHDN overview (https://ecentral.my/e-lejar-lhdn/)
dependencies: Build Monthly e-PCB Plus Submission Reconciliation View

stories.title: Enforce RM1,700 Minimum Wage for ALL Employers Effective 1 August 2025
stories.description: The existing minimum wage story covers the RM1,700 requirement effective February 2025 for MASCO employers and those with 5+ employees. However, from 1 August 2025, the RM1,700 minimum wage universally extends to ALL employers regardless of headcount — including employers with fewer than 5 employees who were previously on the RM1,500 rate. Non-compliance carries fines up to RM10,000 per affected employee per offence. The payroll system must update its minimum wage validation logic to apply RM1,700 universally from August 2025 payroll, removing any employee-count conditional.
acceptanceCriteria: Minimum wage validation in payroll uses RM1,700 as the floor for ALL employees regardless of employer headcount, effective for payroll periods from August 2025 onward
The existing employee-count threshold conditional (5+ employees for RM1,700 vs <5 for RM1,500) is removed from the validation engine for payroll periods >= 2025-08-01
A one-time migration alert notifies HR of any employees currently paid between RM1,500 and RM1,699.99 who will be in violation from August 2025
Payslip submission for August 2025 or later with basic salary below RM1,700 raises a hard validation error citing 'Minimum Wages Order 2024 — universal enforcement from 1 August 2025'
The penalty warning text is updated to reflect RM10,000 per employee fine for non-compliance
technicalNotes: Minimum Wages Order 2024: RM1,700 universal effective 1 August 2025 per MOHR gazette
Previously: RM1,700 applied to MASCO employers from Feb 2025 and employers with 5+ from Feb 2025; <5 non-MASCO employers had until Aug 2025 grace
Foreign workers are covered; domestic workers remain exempt
Reference: MOHR announcement (https://pandahrms.com/rm1700-minimum-wage-applies-to-all-employers-starting-1-august-2025/)
dependencies: Update Minimum Wage Validation to RM1,700 (Feb 2025 Amendment)

stories.title: EPF Mandatory 2% Contribution for Non-Malaysian Citizen Employees (Effective October 2025)
stories.description: The Employees Provident Fund (Amendment) Act 2025, passed in March 2025, mandates EPF contributions for all non-Malaysian citizen employees holding valid employment passes effective 1 October 2025. Both the employer and the employee must contribute 2% of monthly wages each (total 4%). The first payment is due 15 November 2025. Domestic helpers are exempt. The app must add a new contribution tier for foreign workers and update the Borang A / i-Akaun CSV export accordingly. This is a distinct story from the EPF i-Akaun CSV citizen type code story (which tracks the citizen type flag) — this story implements the actual 2% rate calculation.
acceptanceCriteria: Employees flagged as non-Malaysian citizen with a valid employment pass type (VP(TE), EP, Professional VP, Student Pass, Residence Pass, Long-Term Social Visit Pass) have EPF calculated at 2% employee + 2% employer from October 2025 payroll month onward
Domestic helpers (maid, cook, cleaner) are excluded from the 2% mandatory rule even if foreign
Payroll engine uses the employee's nationality flag and work pass type (already on employee record) to determine 2% vs standard Malaysian EPF rates
The i-Akaun employer CSV export correctly labels foreign worker EPF contributions at the 2% statutory rate for the applicable employee rows
A validation error is raised if a non-Malaysian employee with an eligible pass type has EPF rate set to 0% for October 2025 or later payroll
technicalNotes: EPF (Amendment) Act 2025 passed Parliament March 2025; effective 1 October 2025
Contribution rate: 2% employee + 2% employer for non-Malaysian employees (vs 11%+12% or 11%+13% for Malaysians)
First payment due: 15 November 2025 (for October 2025 wages)
Registration of foreign workers via i-Akaun (Employer) is automatic for VP(TE) and EP holders
Reference: KWSP official announcement (https://www.kwsp.gov.my/en/w/news/epf-begins-mandatory-contributions-for-non-malaysian-citizen-employees-effective-october-2025)
dependencies: Generate EPF i-Akaun Electronic Upload File
Add nationality field for foreign worker ISO country code

stories.title: Gig Workers Act 2025 — Non-Platform Contracting Entity SOCSO Deduction Workflow
stories.description: The Gig Workers Act 2025 (Act 872, gazetted December 2025) creates a two-tier obligation: platform providers must register gig workers and deduct SKSPS contributions from earnings; non-platform contracting entities (companies engaging freelancers/gig workers outside a digital platform — e.g., event production companies, construction sub-contractors engaging individuals via service agreements) must also deduct contributions from worker earnings per Section obligations in the Act. The existing PRD stories cover platform provider SKSPS registration and per-transaction contribution — this story covers the non-platform contracting entity workflow with service agreement tracking and aggregate monthly deduction.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Gig Service Agreement' DocType allows recording a non-employee service agreement with a gig worker: worker name, IC/passport, service period, agreed fee, and contracting entity classification (Platform Provider vs Non-Platform Contracting Entity)
For Non-Platform Contracting Entity agreements, the system computes the applicable SOCSO SKSPS deduction from the gig worker's earnings
Monthly aggregate SOCSO deduction across all gig service agreements is compiled into a PERKESO ASSIST-compatible remittance summary
System flags gig service agreements where the contracting entity classification is ambiguous (e.g., a company running its own delivery app) for HR review
Deduction receipts and PERKESO submission references are stored against each gig service agreement for audit
technicalNotes: Gig Workers Act 2025 (Act 872) gazetted December 2025 after receiving Royal Assent 16 December 2025
Non-platform contracting entities include companies engaging individuals under one-off service agreements (acting, filming, events, etc.)
The Act distinguishes platform providers (digital intermediary apps) from other contracting entities — different administrative obligations apply
Reference: Herbert Smith Freehills Kramer analysis (https://www.hsfkramer.com/notes/employment/2025-posts/malaysia-gig-workers-bill-2025)
dependencies: Gig Workers Act 2025 — Auto-Register Gig Workers Under PERKESO SKSPS via ASSIST Portal API

stories.title: SOCSO/EIS New Bracketed Contribution Table at RM6,000 Ceiling (October 2024 Update)
stories.description: PERKESO raised the SOCSO and EIS wage ceiling from RM5,000 to RM6,000 effective 1 October 2024, with a 6-month grace period ending 31 March 2025. The new ceiling means employees earning between RM5,001 and RM6,000 now have higher SOCSO/EIS deductions. Employers with payroll software must update the full Jadual Kadar Caruman (contribution rate schedule) to include the new RM6,001–RM6,000 band. The existing story 'Enforce SOCSO/EIS Wage Ceiling Update to RM6,000' enforces the ceiling cap — this story ensures the bracketed contribution table in the app contains the full published rate rows at the new ceiling tier.
acceptanceCriteria: SOCSO contribution table contains the full PERKESO Jadual Kadar Caruman bands including the RM5,750.01–RM6,000.00 ceiling band with employer rate RM104.15 and employee rate RM29.75
EIS contribution table includes the corresponding ceiling band capped at RM6,000 insured salary
Payroll correctly computes SOCSO for an employee earning RM5,800 (previously uncapped, now in the RM5,750.01–RM6,000 band) as RM104.15 employer + RM29.75 employee
Payroll correctly computes SOCSO for an employee earning RM7,000 using the RM6,000 ceiling row (same RM104.15 + RM29.75 — no change from RM6,000 employee)
The table data is sourced from the official PERKESO Jadual Kadar Caruman effective 1 October 2024 and all 40+ salary bands from RM0–RM30 through RM6,000+ are present
technicalNotes: PERKESO Contribution Rate Table effective 1 October 2024: ceiling raised from RM5,000 to RM6,000
At the RM6,000 ceiling: employer SOCSO = RM104.15, employee SOCSO = RM29.75, total = RM133.90
EIS is also capped at RM6,000 insured salary — employer EIS = RM26.40, employee EIS = RM26.40 at ceiling
Grace period ended 31 March 2025 — all employers must be on the new table from April 2025
Reference: PERKESO rate table (https://www.perkeso.gov.my/en/rate-of-contribution.html)
dependencies: Enforce SOCSO/EIS Wage Ceiling Update to RM6,000 (October 2024 Amendment)
Implement SOCSO Contribution Bracketed Table Lookup (Jadual Kadar Caruman)

stories.title: Generate SOCSO Borang 2 for First-Time Employee PERKESO Registration (ASSIST Portal)
stories.description: PERKESO Borang 2 is the mandatory first-time registration form for new employees who have never previously been registered with SOCSO. Currently the app generates Borang 3 (new employee notification) and Borang 4 (termination). However, for employees without an existing PERKESO number, Borang 2 must be submitted first to obtain the PERKESO reference number before any contributions can be made. Without this, contributions are rejected at PERKESO ASSIST. A separate story exists for Borang 3; this story covers the earlier registration step.
acceptanceCriteria: On Employee save/creation, if `socso_number` is blank, a prompt offers to generate Borang 2 for first-time PERKESO registration
Borang 2 PDF/export contains: employee full name, IC/Passport, date of birth, nationality, gender, address, employer PERKESO number, employment commencement date
After PERKESO assigns a SOCSO reference number (entered manually by HR), the `socso_number` field is populated and Borang 2 status marked as 'Registered'
System prevents generating SOCSO Borang 8A contributions for an employee with blank `socso_number` — must complete Borang 2 first
Borang 2 is generated in PERKESO ASSIST upload format (where applicable) or as a printable PDF for manual submission
technicalNotes: PERKESO Borang 2 is 'Borang Pendaftaran Pekerja Baharu' for first-time employee SOCSO registration
Distinct from Borang 3 (notification of new employee hired, employer obligation within 30 days of hire) — Borang 2 creates the PERKESO member record
Reference: PERKESO ASSIST portal employer guide (https://www.perkeso.gov.my/en/rate-of-contribution.html)
dependencies: Implement SOCSO Borang 3 — New Employee Notification to PERKESO

stories.title: Track Employment Act Section 60D Public Holiday Triple Pay and LHDN PCB Impact
stories.description: Employment Act Section 60D mandates that employees working on a public holiday receive triple pay (3x ORP for work on a gazetted public holiday). This is already covered partially by the 'Implement Public Holiday Work Pay Calculator' story, but the PCB/MTD treatment of the public holiday premium is not covered: the triple pay falls under Section 13(1)(a) employment income and is PCB-liable, must appear in EA Form, and impacts monthly MTD. Additionally, Section 60D covers the alternative of a replacement leave day — if replacement leave is taken instead of the extra pay, no additional PCB applies.
acceptanceCriteria: Public holiday premium pay (2x ORP top-up for working on a gazetted public holiday) is classified as regular employment income under Section 13(1)(a) ITA and included in the PCB base for that month
If the employer and employee agree to replacement leave instead of double premium, the system records the replacement leave grant and does not add taxable income
The public holiday work pay component is separately labelled in the EA Form under the applicable remuneration category
PCB calculation for months with public holiday work premium correctly inflates the gross remuneration for MTD purposes
System validates against a configurable list of Malaysian gazetted public holidays (federal + state) to correctly identify which days qualify
technicalNotes: EA Section 60D: employee working on paid public holiday entitled to: (a) ordinary rate + (b) day's wages at ORP = total 2x ORP extra pay (on top of basic), or replacement leave
The premium pay component is regular employment income (Section 13(1)(a) ITA) — subject to PCB
Replacement leave option avoids additional taxable income — must be documented
Reference: Employment Act 1955 Section 60D and employment law analysis (https://www.zulrafique.com.my)
dependencies: Implement Public Holiday Work Pay Calculator (Section 60D Employment Act) with Replacement Leave Tracking
Implement Ordinary Rate of Pay (ORP) Calculator and Overtime Validation

stories.title: Generate LHDN PCB 2026 Specification Annual Update Checklist and Version Flag
stories.description: LHDN publishes an updated 'Specification for Computerised PCB Calculation' (Spesifikasi Kaedah Pengiraan Berkomputer PCB) each year. For 2025 and 2026, separate specification PDFs exist with different TP1 deduction items, relief ceilings, and MTD formula amendments. The app currently has no mechanism to track which PCB specification version is active, making it difficult for HR administrators to verify that the calculation engine is aligned with the current year's LHDN-published specification. A version flag and annual update checklist is needed.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'PCB Specification Version' setting in LHDN Payroll Settings stores the active specification year (e.g., '2025' or '2026') with a link to the LHDN PDF URL
Switching the specification version triggers a checklist of changed parameters between versions that an HR administrator must review and confirm
The PCB calculation engine reads the active specification year to select the correct TP1 relief ceilings, Schedule 1 tax brackets, and rounding rules for that year
On each payroll run, the system logs which PCB specification version was used — visible in the payroll audit trail
A warning is shown in January each year prompting HR to verify whether LHDN has published an updated specification for the new assessment year
technicalNotes: LHDN 2025 PCB Specification: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/mdahzjwi/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2025.pdf
LHDN 2026 PCB Specification: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf
The 2026 specification confirmed no change to the MTD formula but amended TP1 deduction items
Version management prevents silent errors when LHDN changes reliefs between assessment years
dependencies: Apply YA2025 PCB Schedule 1 Tax Brackets Including New 30% Tier for Income Exceeding RM2 Million

stories.title: Implement Employment Act S.25A Digital Payslip Mandatory Fields Compliance Validator
stories.description: Employment Act Section 25A (as amended by Employment (Amendment) Act 2022) requires employers to provide a payslip to every employee at the time of each wage payment. The payslip must contain mandatory fields including: employee full name, IC/passport number, gender, citizenship status, wage period, wage amount, payment date, overtime earnings, deductions itemised, and employer name. The existing payslip generation may not validate all mandatory fields before generation, exposing employers to MOHR inspection penalties. This story adds a mandatory-field compliance validator to payslip generation.
acceptanceCriteria: Before a Salary Slip is submitted, a validator checks that the generated payslip PDF/print includes: employee full name, IC/passport number, wage period (start and end date), gross salary, itemised deductions (EPF, SOCSO, EIS, PCB, each as separate lines), net pay, and payment date
Missing any mandatory field raises a hard validation error citing 'Employment Act S.25A — Payslip mandatory fields'
The payslip print template is updated to include gender and citizenship status fields if currently absent
A payslip compliance audit report lists any historical salary slips missing mandatory fields
The system confirms LHDN statutory deductions (PCB, EPF, SOCSO, EIS) are itemised individually on the payslip — not combined into a single 'deductions' total
technicalNotes: Employment Act S.25A (post-2022 amendment): employer must give payslip at every wage payment
MOHR inspection can impose fines for non-compliant payslips
Mandatory fields per EA S.25A: name, employee ID, gender, citizenship, wage period, amount, date, all payments/deductions with particulars
Reference: Employment Act 1955 (Akta 265) Section 25A (https://jtksm.mohr.gov.my/sites/default/files/2023-11/Akta%20Kerja%201955%20(Akta%20265)_0.pdf)
dependencies: Generate Employment Act S.61 Compliant Payslip with All Mandatory Fields

stories.title: PDPA 2024 Data Portability and Payroll Employee Data Export on Request
stories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (gazetted and phased in January–June 2025) introduced a new data portability right: employees (data subjects) can request their personal data be transferred from the employer (data controller) to another party in a machine-readable format within a prescribed period. For payroll, this means employees can request an export of all their payroll data (payslips, EA forms, PCB deductions, leave records, statutory contribution history) in a portable format. The existing PDPA story covers DPO appointment and breach notification — this story covers the portability right specifically for payroll records.
acceptanceCriteria: An employee self-service action 'Request My Payroll Data Export' generates a structured export (JSON or CSV) of all the requesting employee's payroll records within the system
The export includes: all payslips, EA forms, TP1/TP2/TP3 records, EPF/SOCSO/EIS contributions per month, PCB deductions, and leave records for the current and prior 7 years
The export is generated within 7 days of request (system setting for response SLA) and is made available for download via a secure, time-limited link
A 'Data Subject Request Log' records who requested data, when, and when/how it was fulfilled — retained for audit
The export format is machine-readable (JSON preferred) and human-readable (PDF summary) — both generated simultaneously
technicalNotes: PDPA Amendment Act 2024 passed 31 July 2024; portability right phased in by June 2025
Portability right applies where processing is by automated means and based on consent or contract
Employment relationship creates a contract basis — payroll data qualifies for portability

Reference: Mayer Brown PDPA analysis (https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2025/07/from-legislative-reform-to-practical-guidance-key-amendments-to-malaysias-pdpa-and-the-launch-of-cross-border-transfer-guidelines)
dependencies: PDPA 2024 DPO Appointment Registry and 72-Hour Payroll Data Breach Notification Workflow
Build Employee Self-Service Portal for Payslips, EA Forms, and TP1 Submission
